critique.summary: The article explores the new customizable <select> feature available in recent Chromium-based browsers, demonstrating how it enables advanced styling and animation of select elements using CSS. It provides detailed examples including a curved stack of folders, a fanned deck of cards, and a radial emoji picker, showcasing the potential of this feature for creative UI design.
mainPoints: Customizable selects allow embedding HTML elements inside <option> tags, enhancing styling possibilities beyond plain text.
The ::picker() pseudo-element and related CSS features enable full control over the appearance and behavior of select dropdowns.
New CSS functions like sibling-index(), sibling-count(), and anchor() facilitate dynamic positioning and animation of options.
The feature currently works only in recent Chromium-based browsers but degrades gracefully in unsupported browsers.
The article provides three demos: a curved stack of folders, a fanned deck of cards, and a radial emoji picker, each illustrating different uses of customizable selects.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the current state of customizable select elements as implemented in Chromium-based browsers, including the use of new CSS pseudo-elements and functions. The claims about browser support, CSS capabilities, and the progressive enhancement nature of the feature are consistent with official specifications and recent browser updates. The demos and code snippets align with known experimental CSS features and do not contain factual errors.
considerations: The article does not mention that these features are experimental and may change or require enabling flags in browsers.
It does not discuss accessibility implications in detail beyond noting that the select remains accessible.
There is no mention of support or plans for non-Chromium browsers like Firefox or Safari.
Performance considerations or potential limitations when using complex animations in selects are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion appreciates the creativity behind customizable selects but also expresses concern about potential overuse leading to cluttered designs. There is a hope for tools that can simplify or standardize such customizations for better usability.

comments.author: love2read
comments.text: The person who wrote this post is incredibly creative. Every one of these is more interesting than 10 google tools. I guess thus is the rule of industrial tools, that is, to minimize surprise.
comments.analysis: This comment positively acknowledges the creativity and innovation in the article, which is a reasonable and accurate appreciation of the content.

comments.author: duskdozer
comments.text: I admit this is neat tech but I am not looking forward to the monstrosities that are going to result. I'm hoping there will be (already is?) something that could translate anything into a stripped down minimal-effect version. Like reader mode but universal
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced perspective, recognizing the technology's novelty while cautioning about potential negative outcomes, which is a valid concern in UI design.
